claims.claim: JavaScript Allongé is a book about programming with functions, emphasizing how functions flow into many ideas like decorators, methods, delegation, and mixins.
claims.evidence: JavaScript Allongé is a book about programming with functions. From functions flow many ideas, from decorators to methods to delegation to mixins, and onwards in so many fruitful directions.

claims.claim: JavaScript Allongé focuses on fundamental programming ideas and how they combine to form new concepts, aiming to improve how we think about programs.
claims.evidence: The focus in this book on the underlying ideas, what we might call the fundamentals, and how they combine to form new ideas. The intention is to improve the way we think about programs.

claims.claim: JavaScript Allongé is not prescriptive; it does not suggest that any techniques shown are the only, best, or acceptable way to write programs.
claims.evidence: But while JavaScript Allongé attempts to be provocative, it is not _prescriptive_. There is absolutely no suggestion that any of the techniques shown here are the only way to do something, the best way, or even an acceptable way to write programs that are intended to be used, read, and maintained by others.

claims.claim: Programming choices are influenced by social considerations, requirements of the software type, and consistency within a codebase.
claims.evidence: Choices in software development are also often driven by requirements specific to the type of software being developed. For example, business software written in-house has a very different set of requirements than a library written to be publicly distributed as open-source. Choices in software development must also consider the question of consistency.

claims.claim: Software development choices must also consider tooling such as source-code control systems, linters, debuggers, and continuous integration.
claims.evidence: Choices in software development cannot ignore the tooling that is used to create and maintain software. The use of source-code control systems with integrated diffing rewards making certain types of focused changes. The use of linters makes checking for certain types of undesirable code very cheap. Debuggers encourage the use of functions with explicit or implicit names. Continuous integration encourages the creation of software in tandem with and factored to facilitate the creation of automated test suites.

claims.claim: JavaScript Allongé is organized to introduce new aspects of programming with functions in each chapter, with code examples emphasizing exposition.
claims.evidence: _JavaScript Allongé_ introduces new aspects of programming with functions in each chapter, explaining exactly how JavaScript works. Code examples within each chapter are small and emphasize exposition rather than serving as patterns for everyday use.

claims.claim: ECMAScript 2015 (ES6) introduced features that make programming with functions easier and more expressive.
claims.evidence: ECMAScript 2015 (formerly called ECMAScript 6 or "ES6"), is ushering in a very large number of improvements to the way programmers can write small, powerful components and combine them into larger, fully featured programs.

claims.claim: Prior to ES6, JavaScript lacked block-structured variables, requiring workarounds like IIFEs to achieve similar scoping.
claims.evidence: Prior to ECMAScript 2015, JavaScript did not support block-structuring, so programmers borrowed a trick from the Scheme programming language, and would write: var i; for (i = 0; i < array.length; ++i) { (function (i) { // ... })(i) }

claims.claim: Before ES6, JavaScript did not support collecting a variable number of arguments into a parameter, requiring manual slicing of arguments.
claims.evidence: Prior to ECMAScript 2015, JavaScript did not support collecting a variable number of arguments into a parameter, so programmers would take advantage of an awkward work-around and write things like: function foo () { var first = arguments[0], rest = [].slice.call(arguments, 1); // ... }

claims.claim: ES6 introduced let for block-scoped variables and ...rest for collecting variable arguments, simplifying function syntax.
claims.evidence: We can also write: function foo (first, ...rest) { // ... } And presto, rest collects the rest of the arguments without a lot of malarky involving slicing arguments.

claims.claim: The 'Six' edition of JavaScript Allongé introduces classes, mixins, private properties with symbols, iterators, and generators.
claims.evidence: Thus, the "six" edition introduces classes and mixins. It introduces the notion of implementing private properties with symbols. It introduces iterators and generators.

claims.claim: The 'Six' edition emphasizes that all these new features can still be approached using the same 'programming with functions' techniques.
claims.evidence: But the common thread that runs through all these things is that since they are all simple objects and simple functions, we can use the same set of "programming with functions" techniques to build programs by composing small, flexible, and decoupled entities.

claims.claim: In JavaScript, all values are expressions, meaning they can be used directly in code to produce results.
claims.evidence: All values are expressions. Say you hand the barista a café Cubano. Yup, you hand over a cup with some coffee infused through partially caramelized sugar. You say, "I want one of these." The barista is no fool, she gives it straight back to you, and you get exactly what you want.

claims.claim: Expressions that combine values with operators are not values themselves but represent operations that evaluate to values.
claims.evidence: Boiling water plus ground coffee is an expression, but it isn't a value. Boiling water is a value. Ground coffee is a value. Espresso is a value. Boiling water plus ground coffee is an expression.

claims.claim: JavaScript uses the === operator to test if two values are identical and !== to test if they are not identical.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, we test whether two values are identical with the === operator, and whether they are not identical with the !== operator:

claims.claim: Two values in JavaScript are not identical if they have different types, such as a string versus a number.
claims.evidence: First, sometimes, the cups are of different kinds. One is a demitasse, the other a mug. This corresponds to comparing two things in JavaScript that have different _types_ . For example, the string "2" is not the same thing as the number 2. Strings and numbers are different types, so strings and numbers are never identical:

claims.claim: Two values in JavaScript are not identical if they have the same type but different content.
claims.evidence: Second, sometimes, the cups are of the same type-perhaps two espresso cups-but they have different contents. One holds a single, one a double. This corresponds to comparing two JavaScript values that have the same type but different "content." For example, the number 5 is not the same thing as the number 2.

claims.claim: ECMAScript 2015 introduced three major groups of features: better syntax for existing features, new functionality in the standard library, and completely new features.
claims.evidence: ECMAScript 6 has three major groups of features: - Better syntax for features that already exist (e.g. via libraries). For example: classes and modules. - New functionality in the standard library. For example: New methods for strings and arrays - Promises (for asynchronous programming) - Maps and sets - Completely new features. For example: Generators, proxies and WeakMaps.

claims.claim: ECMAScript 2015 was ratified as a standard on June 17, 2015, after years of development and compromise.
claims.evidence: ECMAScript 6 (short name: ES6; official name: ECMAScript 2015) was ratified as a standard on June 17. Getting there took a while - in a way, the origins of ES6 date back to the year 2000: After ECMAScript 3 was finished, TC39 (the committee evolving JavaScript) started to work on ECMAScript 4.

claims.claim: All values in JavaScript are also expressions.
claims.evidence: All values are expressions. That's easy!

claims.claim: Some expressions are values, but not all expressions are values.
claims.evidence: Boiling water plus ground coffee is an expression. But it isn't a value.

claims.claim: Strings are values and also expressions by themselves.
claims.evidence: Since strings are values, they are also expressions by themselves.

claims.claim: Expressions combining strings with operators are not values, but are expressions.
claims.evidence: But strings with operators are not values, they are expressions.

claims.claim: JavaScript uses the === operator to test if two values are identical.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, we test whether two values are identical with the === operator

claims.claim: Two values are not identical if they have different types.
claims.evidence: One is a demitasse, the other a mug. This corresponds to comparing two things in JavaScript that have different _types_.

claims.claim: Two values of the same type but different content are not identical.
claims.evidence: Second, sometimes, the cups are of the same type-perhaps two espresso cups-but they have different contents.

claims.claim: Values of primitive types like strings, numbers, and booleans are identical if they have the same type and content.
claims.evidence: Strings, numbers, and booleans are examples of what JavaScript calls "value" or "primitive" types.

claims.claim: Arrays are reference types, not value types, so identical-looking arrays are not identical.
claims.evidence: Every time you evaluate an expression (including typing something in) to create an array, you're creating a new, distinct value even if it _appears_ to be the same as some other array value.

claims.claim: Functions are reference types, so identical-looking functions are not identical.
claims.evidence: Like arrays, every time you evaluate an expression to produce a function, you get a new function that is not identical to any other function, even if you use the same expression to generate it.

claims.claim: The undefined value represents the absence of a value.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, the absence of a value is written undefined, and it means there is no value.

claims.claim: The undefined value is identical to every other undefined value.
claims.evidence: undefined is its own type of value, and it acts like a value type: undefined === undefined //=> true

claims.claim: The void operator always evaluates to undefined regardless of input.
claims.evidence: void is an operator that takes any value and evaluates to undefined, always.

claims.claim: Functions in JavaScript are values.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, functions are values, but they are also much more than simple numbers, strings, or even complex data structures like trees or maps.

claims.claim: Functions can be applied to zero or more arguments to produce a value.
claims.evidence: Let's put functions to work. The way we use functions is to _apply_ them to zero or more values called _arguments_.

claims.claim: Functions can return values or evaluate expressions.
claims.evidence: We can make a function that returns a value by putting the value to the right of the arrow.

claims.claim: Functions can return the result of evaluating another function.
claims.evidence: Yes we can! Functions can return the value of evaluating another function.

claims.claim: The comma operator evaluates both arguments and returns the value of the right-hand argument.
claims.evidence: The comma operator in JavaScript is interesting. It takes two arguments, evaluates them both, and itself evaluates to the value of the right-hand argument.

claims.claim: A function with an empty block returns undefined when applied.
A function with a block containing expressions evaluates to undefined unless a return statement is used.
The return statement immediately terminates function execution and returns the result of its expression.
claims.evidence: The return keyword creates a _return statement_ that immediately terminates the function application and returns the result of evaluating its expression.

claims.claim: Statements cannot be used as expressions in function bodies.
claims.evidence: Statements belong inside blocks and only inside blocks. Some languages simplify this by making everything an expression, but JavaScript maintains this distinction.

claims.claim: Functions can be returned from other functions.
claims.evidence: Can we put an expression that evaluates to a function on the right side of a function expression?

claims.claim: JavaScript literals represent fixed values in source code.
claims.evidence: In computer science, a literal is a notation for representing a fixed value in source code.

claims.claim: Numbers in JavaScript can be represented in different bases.
claims.evidence: If we start a literal with a zero, it is an octal literal. So the literal 042 is 42 base 8, which is actually 34 base 10.

claims.claim: JavaScript numbers are internally represented as double-precision floating-point values.
claims.evidence: Internally, both 042 and 34 have the same representation, as double-precision floating point numbers.

claims.claim: JavaScript has a limit on the largest safe integer it can handle.
claims.evidence: The largest integer JavaScript can safely handle is 9007199254740991, or 2^53 - 1.

claims.claim: Floating-point numbers in JavaScript can have inexact representations due to binary vs decimal conversion.
claims.evidence: This makes no meaningful difference for integers, but it does for fractions, because some fractions base 10 do not have exact representations base 2.

claims.claim: JavaScript performs arithmetic operations according to operator precedence.
claims.evidence: JavaScript has many more operators. In a sense, they behave like little functions. If we write 1 + 2, this is conceptually similar to writing plus(1, 2).

claims.claim: JavaScript supports various numeric operators including modulus and unary negation.
claims.evidence: In addition to the common +, -, *, and /, JavaScript also supports modulus, %, and unary negation, -:

claims.claim: JavaScript functions are values with a representation.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, functions are values, but they are also much more than simple numbers, strings, or even complex data structures like trees or maps.

claims.claim: A function block with one or more expressions evaluates to undefined
A function with a single expression evaluates to that expression's value
A function block with multiple expressions separated by commas evaluates to the last expression's value
A function block with multiple expressions evaluates to undefined unless a return statement is used
Using the return keyword in a function block allows returning a specific value
The return statement immediately terminates function execution and returns the result of its expression
A return statement cannot be used as an expression in a simple function
Functions can be nested to return other functions
Nested functions can be applied multiple times to produce a chain of evaluations
Functions can accept arguments and use them in their bodies
claims.evidence: (diameter) => diameter * 3.14159265

claims.claim: Functions with arguments are applied by placing arguments in parentheses after the function
claims.evidence: ((diameter) => diameter * 3.14159265)(2)

claims.claim: Functions can have multiple arguments
claims.evidence: (room, board) => {}

claims.claim: Functions with multiple arguments are applied by providing all arguments in parentheses
claims.evidence: ((room, board) => room + board)(800, 150)

claims.claim: JavaScript uses call by value evaluation strategy
claims.evidence: JavaScript uses the "call by value" evaluation strategy

claims.claim: In call by value, arguments are evaluated before being passed to a function
claims.evidence: JavaScript evaluates all of those expressions and applies the functions to the resulting value(s)

claims.claim: When a function is invoked, a new environment is created mapping variables to values
claims.evidence: Every time a function is invoked, a new _environment_ is created

claims.claim: Arguments are bound to names in the function's environment upon invocation
claims.evidence: When you apply the function to the arguments, an entry is placed in the dictionary for each argument

claims.claim: Variables in a function's environment refer to values bound to those names
claims.evidence: The value of a variable when evaluated in an environment is the value bound to the variable's name in that environment

claims.claim: JavaScript implements call by sharing semantics for reference types
claims.evidence: JavaScript can be said to implement "call by sharing" semantics

claims.claim: Call by sharing ensures that values passed as arguments remain identical to originals
claims.evidence: When a value-any value-is passed as an argument to a function, the value bound in the function's environment must be identical to the original

claims.claim: Value types are copied when bound to names in environments
claims.evidence: When JavaScript binds a value-type to a name, it makes a copy of the value and places the copy in the environment

claims.claim: Reference types are referenced rather than copied in environments
claims.evidence: JavaScript places _references_ to reference types in environments

claims.claim: A closure is a function that contains a free variable not bound within its own scope
claims.evidence: Functions containing one or more free variables are called _closures_

claims.claim: Free variables in closures are resolved through parent environments
claims.evidence: The environment for ((y) => x)(2) is _actually_ {y: 2, '..': {x: 1, ...}}

claims.claim: Closures can access variables from enclosing scopes
claims.evidence: Whenever a function is applied to arguments, its environment always has a reference to its parent environment

claims.claim: Variables with the same name in nested scopes shadow outer variables
claims.evidence: When a variable has the same name as an ancestor environment's binding, it is said to _shadow_ the ancestor

claims.claim: Pure functions contain no free variables and always behave consistently
claims.evidence: Functions containing no free variables are called _pure functions_

claims.claim: Closures can be composed within pure functions
claims.evidence: From this, we learn something: A pure function can contain a closure

claims.claim: Functions can be named using immediately invoked function expressions
claims.evidence: ((PI) => (diameter) => diameter * PI )(3.14159265)

claims.claim: Variables can be bound to names using immediately invoked functions
claims.evidence: We can bind anything we want in an expression by wrapping it in a function that is immediately invoked with the value we want to bind

claims.claim: Functions in JavaScript are first-class entities that can be bound to names, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.
claims.evidence: Amazing how such an important idea-naming functions-can be explained _en passant_ in just a few words. That emphasizes one of the things JavaScript gets really, really right: Functions as "first class entities." Functions are values that can be bound to names like any other value, passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and so forth.

claims.claim: Anonymous functions can be named by binding them to identifiers using various techniques including Immediately Invoked Function Expressions (IIFE).
claims.evidence: Up to now, all we've really seen are _anonymous functions_ , functions that don't have a name. This feels very different from programming in most other languages, where the focus is on naming functions, methods, and procedures. Naming things is a critical part of programming, but all we've seen so far is how to name arguments.

claims.claim: A function can be created by wrapping an expression in parentheses and immediately invoking it with arguments to bind values to names.
claims.evidence: In order to bind 3.14159265 to the name PI, we'll need a function with a parameter of PI applied to an argument of 3.14159265. If we put our function expression in parentheses, we can apply it to the argument of 3.14159265: ((PI) => _// ????_ )(3.14159265)

claims.claim: An IIFE can be used to bind a value to a name and then return a function that uses that name.
claims.evidence: ((PI) => (diameter) => diameter * PI )(3.14159265)

claims.claim: Functions can be written using either arrow syntax or the function keyword.
claims.evidence: Here's our repeat function written using a "fat arrow" (str) => str + str And here's (almost) the exact same function written using the function keyword: function (str) { return str + str }

claims.claim: Named function expressions allow a function to have an internal name distinct from the identifier it is bound to.
claims.evidence: Placing a name between the function keyword and the argument list names the function. Confusingly, the name of the function is not exactly the same thing as the name we may choose to bind to the value of the function.

claims.claim: The name of a function defined with the function keyword is accessible via the .name property.
claims.evidence: double.name //=> 'repeat'

claims.claim: A function defined with the function keyword can reference itself by name within its own body, enabling recursion.
claims.evidence: (function even (n) { if (n === 0) { return true } else return !even(n - 1) })(5) //=> false

claims.claim: Function declarations are statements that define functions and can be hoisted to the top of their scope.
claims.evidence: There is another syntax for naming and/or defining a function. It's called a _function declaration statement_ , and it looks a lot like a named function expression, only we use it as a statement: function someName () { // ... }

claims.claim: Function declarations behave similarly to named function expressions when bound to identifiers.
claims.evidence: This behaves a _little_ like: const someName = function someName () {

claims.claim: Closures capture variables from their enclosing scope at the time of creation, not at the time of execution.
claims.evidence: We know this from the chapter on closures, but even though PI is not bound when we invoke diameter_fn by evaluating diameter_fn(2), PI _is_ bound when we evaluated (diameter) => diameter * PI, and thus the expression diameter * PI is able to access values for PI and diameter when we evaluate diameter_fn.

claims.claim: Variables bound with const follow lexical scoping rules just like parameters in closures.
claims.evidence: Yes. Binding values to names with const works just like binding values to names with parameter invocations, it uses lexical scope.

claims.claim: When a variable is bound with const in an inner scope, it shadows the outer binding without affecting it.
claims.evidence: We say that when we bind a variable using a parameter inside another binding, the inner binding _shadows_ the outer binding. It has effect inside its own scope, but does not affect the binding in the enclosing scope.

claims.claim: Variables bound with const in nested blocks shadow outer bindings, ensuring proper lexical scoping.
claims.evidence: Ah! const statements don't just shadow values bound within the environments created by functions, they shadow values bound within environments created by blocks!

claims.claim: The const keyword allows binding values to names within blocks without incurring function invocation overhead.
claims.evidence: The const keyword introduces one or more bindings in the block that encloses it. It doesn't incur the cost of a function invocation.

claims.claim: Multiple constants can be declared in a single const statement separated by commas.
claims.evidence: We can bind more than one name-value pair by separating them with commas. For readability, most people put one binding per line:

claims.claim: Constants declared with const cannot be reassigned after initial binding.
claims.evidence: JavaScript does not permit us to rebind a name that has been bound with const.

claims.claim: Blocks can contain const declarations and these create new scopes for the declared variables.
claims.evidence: const statements occur inside blocks, we can't use them when we write a fat arrow that has an expression as its body.

claims.claim: If statements can contain blocks which themselves can contain const declarations.
claims.evidence: One of the places you can find blocks is in an if statement. In JavaScript, an if statement looks like this: (n) => { const even = (x) => { if (x === 0) return true; else return !even(x - 1); } return even(n) }

claims.claim: Functions are expressions and can be bound to identifiers just like other values.
claims.evidence: Functions are values that can be bound to names like any other value, passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and so forth.

claims.claim: Every value in JavaScript is either truthy or falsy, and logical operators operate on truthiness rather than strict boolean values.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, there is a notion of "truthiness." Every value is either "truthy" or "falsy." Obviously, false is falsy. So are null and undefined, values that semantically represent "no value." NaN is falsy, a value representing the result of a calculation that is not a number. And there are more: 0 is falsy, a value representing "none of something." The empty string, '' is falsy, a value representing having no characters.

claims.claim: The ternary operator is an expression that evaluates to either the second or third operand based on the truthiness of the first operand.
claims.evidence: JavaScript inherited an operator from the C family of languages, the _ternary_ operator. It's the only operator that takes _three_ arguments. It looks like this: first ? second : third. It evaluates first, and if first is "truthy", it evaluates second and that is its value. If first is not truthy, it evaluates third and that is its value.

claims.claim: The logical NOT operator (!) always returns true or false based on the truthiness of its argument.
claims.evidence: ! is the simplest. It always returns false if its argument is truthy, and true is its argument is not truthy:

claims.claim: The logical AND (&&) and OR (||) operators do not necessarily return true or false, but instead return the value of one of their operands based on truthiness.
claims.evidence: && and || do not necessarily evaluate to true or false. To be precise: - && evaluates its left-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something falsy, && returns the value of its lefthand expression without evaluating its right-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something truthy, && evaluates its right-hand expression and returns the value of the right-hand expression. - || evaluates its left-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something truthy, || returns the value of its lefthand expression without evaluating its right-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something false, || evaluates its right-hand expression and returns the value of the right-hand expression.

claims.claim: The logical AND (&&) and OR (||) operators have short-circuit semantics, meaning they may not evaluate the right-hand side depending on the left-hand side.
claims.evidence: If we look at our examples above, we see that when we pass true and false to && and ||, we do indeed get true or false as a result. But when we pass other values, we no longer get true or false: 1 || 2 _//=> 1_ null && undefined _//=> null_ undefined && null _//=> undefined

claims.claim: The logical AND (&&) and OR (||) operators are control-flow operators, not logical operators in the traditional sense.
claims.evidence: We've seen the ternary operator: It is a _control-flow_ operator, not a logical operator. The same is true of && and ||. Consider this tail-recursive function that determines whether a positive integer is even: For example: 75 Picking the Bean: Choice and Truthiness const even = (n) => n === 0 || (n !== 1 && even(n - 2))

claims.claim: Function parameters are eagerly evaluated, which can cause issues with control-flow operators that should defer evaluation.
claims.evidence: In contrast to the behaviour of the ternary operator, ||, and &&, function parameters are always : _eagerly evaluated_

claims.claim: To implement control-flow semantics in functions, pass functions instead of expressions to delay evaluation.
claims.evidence: If we need to have functions with control-flow semantics, we can pass anonymous functions. We obviously don't need anything like this for or and and, but to demonstrate the technique: const or = (a, b) => a() || b() const and = (a, b) => a() && b() const even = (n) => or(() => n === 0, () => and(() => n !== 1, () => even(n - 2))) even(7) _//=> false_

claims.claim: Array literals in JavaScript are expressions that create new, distinct arrays each time they are evaluated.
claims.evidence: Array literals are expressions, and arrays are _reference types_ . We can see that each time an array literal is evaluated, we get a new, distinct array, even if it contains the exact same elements:

claims.claim: Array elements are stored as references to the values placed in them, not copies.
claims.evidence: a[0] === x _//=> true, arrays store references to the things you put in them.

claims.claim: Array destructuring allows extracting elements from arrays using patterns that mirror array literals.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, we can actually _reverse_ the statement and place the template on the left and a value on the right: const unwrap = (wrapped) => { const [something] = wrapped; return something; }

claims.claim: Destructuring supports nested patterns to extract elements from complex array structures.
claims.evidence: Destructuring can nest: const description = (nameAndOccupation) => { const [[first, last], occupation] = nameAndOccupation; return ` ${first} is a ${occupation} `; }

claims.claim: The spread operator (...) can be used to gather remaining elements into an array during destructuring.
claims.evidence: Extracting the head and gathering everything but the head from an array: const [car, ...cdr] = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

claims.claim: The spread operator (...) can be used to insert elements of one array into another array literal.
claims.evidence: It works! We can use ... to place the elements of an array inside another array. We say that using ... to destructure is gathering, and using it in a literal to insert elements is called "spreading."

claims.claim: JavaScript's array destructuring does not perform pattern matching like some other languages; it assigns undefined when there are insufficient elements.
claims.evidence: In such a language, if you wrote something like: const [what] = []; That match would fail because the array doesn't have an element to assign to what. But this is not how JavaScript works. JavaScript tries its best to assign things, and if there isn't something that fits, JavaScript binds undefined to the name.

claims.claim: Destructuring can be used to simulate multiple return values from functions by returning an array.
claims.evidence: Some languages support multiple return values: A function can return several things at once, like a value and an error code. This can easily be emulated in JavaScript with destructuring:

claims.claim: Function parameters support gathering using the spread operator to collect remaining arguments into an array.
claims.evidence: Gathering works with parameters! This is very useful indeed, and we'll see more of it in a moment. const numbers = (...nums) => nums; numbers(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) _//=> [1,2,3,4,5] const headAndTail = (head, ...tail) => [head, tail]; headAndTail(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) _//=> [1,[2,3,4,5]]

claims.claim: Arrays can be defined recursively using the concept of a list being either empty or an element followed by another list.
claims.evidence: One of the simplest, and longeststanding in computer science, is to say that a list is: 1. Empty, or; 2. Consists of an element concatenated with a list .

claims.claim: Array literals combined with the spread operator mirror the behavior of destructuring with rest parameters.
claims.evidence: Let's convert our rules to array literals. The first rule is simple: [] is a list. How about the second rule? We can express that using a spread. Given an element e and a list list, [e, ...list] is a list.

claims.claim: A function can use default arguments to provide fallback values for parameters when they are not supplied.
claims.evidence: const factorial = (n, work = 1) => n === 1 ? work : factorial(n - 1, n * work);

claims.claim: Default arguments allow functions to be called with fewer arguments than defined, providing automatic fallback values.
claims.evidence: By writing our parameter list as (n, work = 1) =>, we're stating that if a second parameter is not provided, work is to be bound to 1.

claims.claim: Functions can be designed to accept a function as an argument, enabling higher-order functions.
claims.evidence: Functions can take functions as arguments, so let's "extract" the thing to do to each element and separate it from the business of taking an array apart, doing the thing, and putting the array back together.

claims.claim: Arrays can be defined recursively as either empty or consisting of an element concatenated with another array.
claims.evidence: A list is: 1. Empty, or; 2. Consists of an element concatenated with a list.

claims.claim: Array literals and spreads can be used to build arrays in a way that mirrors destructuring with rest parameters.
claims.evidence: We can express that using a spread. Given an element e and a list list, [e, ...list] is a list.

claims.claim: Destructuring with rest parameters allows decomposition of arrays into first element and remaining elements.
claims.evidence: const [first, ...rest] = ["foo", "bar", "baz"]; first //=> "foo" rest //=> ["bar","baz"]

claims.claim: An empty array can be tested for using destructuring with rest parameters to check if the first element is undefined.
claims.evidence: const isEmpty = ([first, ...rest]) => first === undefined ;

claims.claim: Recursive functions can be used to calculate the length of an array by breaking it down into smaller parts until reaching an empty array.
claims.evidence: const length = ([first, ...rest]) => first === undefined ? 0 : 1 + length(rest);

claims.claim: Linear recursion involves dividing a problem into smaller subproblems, solving terminal cases, and combining results.
claims.evidence: Linear recursion is very useful and simple to understand. Let's take another example. Sometimes we want to flatten an array...

claims.claim: Recursive algorithms follow a divide and conquer strategy: divide the problem into smaller problems, solve small problems, and compose solutions.
claims.evidence: Recursive algorithms follow the "divide and conquer" strategy for solving a problem: 1. Divide the problem into smaller problems 2. If a smaller problem is solvable, solve the small problem 3. If a smaller problem is not solvable, divide and conquer that problem 4. When all small problems have been solved, compose the solutions into one big solution

claims.claim: Flattening an array can be implemented recursively by checking if elements are arrays and handling them accordingly.
claims.evidence: const flatten = ([first, ...rest]) => { if (first === undefined ) { return []; } else if (!Array.isArray(first)) { return [first, ...flatten(rest)]; } else { return [...flatten(first), ...flatten(rest)]; } }

claims.claim: Mapping over arrays can be implemented recursively by applying a function to each element and combining results.
claims.evidence: const squareAll = ([first, ...rest]) => first === undefined ? [] : [first * first, ...squareAll(rest)];

claims.claim: The mapWith function applies a given function to each element of an array using recursion.
claims.evidence: const mapWith = (fn, [first, ...rest]) => first === undefined ? [] : [fn(first), ...mapWith(fn, rest)];

claims.claim: Folding is a generalization of mapping that combines elements using a function and a terminal value.
claims.evidence: const foldWith = (fn, terminalValue, [first, ...rest]) => first === undefined ? terminalValue : fn(first, foldWith(fn, terminalValue, rest));

claims.claim: Length can be computed using a fold operation with a terminal value of 0 and a function that adds 1.
claims.evidence: const length = (array) => foldWith((first, rest) => 1 + rest, 0, array);

claims.claim: The mapWith function can be expressed in terms of foldWith by supplying appropriate combining logic.
claims.evidence: const mapWith = (fn, array) => foldWith((first, rest) => [fn(first), ...rest], [], array)

claims.claim: Tail-call optimization allows recursive functions to avoid accumulating stack frames by making the recursive call the final action of the function.
claims.evidence: If a function makes a call in tail position, JavaScript optimizes away the function call overhead and stack space.

claims.claim: Non-tail-recursive functions accumulate stack frames and can cause memory issues with large inputs.
claims.evidence: One of the reasons they are not production-ready is that they consume memory proportional to the size of the array being folded.

claims.claim: Converting a non-tail-recursive function to a tail-recursive one involves passing accumulated work as an additional parameter.
claims.evidence: const lengthDelaysWork = ([first, ...rest], numberToBeAdded) => first === undefined ? numberToBeAdded : lengthDelaysWork(rest, 1 + numberToBeAdded)

claims.claim: Tail-recursive functions can handle large datasets efficiently by avoiding stack overflow through tail-call optimization.
claims.evidence: This version of length calls uses lengthDelaysWork, and JavaScript optimizes that not to take up memory proportional to the length of the string.

claims.claim: Mapping is a specialized form of recursion where a function is applied to each element of a data structure.
claims.evidence: This specific case of linear recursion is called "mapping," and it is not necessary to constantly write out the same pattern again and again.

claims.claim: Folding is a generalization of mapping that combines elements using a function and a terminal value.
claims.evidence: Our foldWith function is a generalization of our mapWith function.

claims.claim: Functions can be composed to create new functions that perform complex operations.
claims.evidence: We can represent a map as a fold, we just need to supply the array rebuilding code:

claims.claim: Destructuring parameters works similarly to destructuring assignment and supports default values.
claims.evidence: We saw earlier that destructuring parameters works the same way as destructuring assignment. Now we learn that we can create a default parameter argument.

claims.claim: A function can be created by partially applying another function with some arguments using a helper like callLast.
claims.evidence: const callLast = (fn, ...args) => (...remainingArgs) => fn(...remainingArgs, ...args);

claims.claim: Partial application allows creating a new function with some arguments pre-filled.
claims.evidence: const factorial = callLast(factorialWithDelayedWork, 1);

claims.claim: Default arguments provide fallback values for function parameters when they are not supplied.
claims.evidence: const factorial = (n, work = 1) => n === 1 ? work : factorial(n - 1, n * work);

claims.claim: Default arguments can be used with destructuring patterns to provide fallback values.
claims.evidence: const [first, second = "two"] = ["one"];

claims.claim: Default arguments improve readability by hiding implementation details from users of a function.
claims.evidence: But it is hideous to have to always add a 1 parameter, we'd be demanding that everyone using the factorial function know that we are using a tail-recursive implementation.

claims.claim: Using array destructuring with rest syntax to split arrays creates temporary arrays which leads to performance issues.
claims.evidence: Every time we call mapWith, we're calling [...prepend, fn(first)]. To do that, we take the array in prepend and push fn(first) onto the end, creating a new array that will be passed to the next invocation of mapWith.

claims.claim: Repeated creation and copying of arrays during recursive operations causes significant overhead and memory thrashing.
claims.evidence: We may not be creating 3,000 stack frames, but we are creating three thousand new arrays and copying elements into each and every one of them.

claims.claim: JavaScript objects are mappings from string keys to values.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, an object is a map from string keys to values.

claims.claim: Objects can be created using literal syntax with key-value pairs enclosed in curly braces.
claims.evidence: { year: 2012, month: 6, day: 14 }

claims.claim: Object properties can be accessed using bracket notation with string keys.
claims.evidence: { year: 2012, month: 6, day: 14 }['day']

claims.claim: Property access using dot notation is equivalent to bracket notation when property names are valid identifiers.
claims.evidence: date['day'] === date.day

claims.claim: Object keys can be computed expressions enclosed in square brackets.
Functions can be assigned as values to object properties using compact method syntax.
claims.evidence: encode (plaintext) { return plaintext .split('') .map( char => char .charCodeAt() ) .map( code => code + 1 ) .map( code => String.fromCharCode(code) ) .join(''); }

claims.claim: JavaScript objects can contain any type of value including functions and other objects.
claims.evidence: All containers can contain any value, including functions or other containers, like a fat arrow function:

claims.claim: Tail recursion can be used to implement iterative algorithms using recursive functions without growing the call stack.
claims.evidence: Our problem is that we can directly write: const factorial = (n, work) => n === 1 ? work : factorial(n - 1, n * work);

claims.claim: Tail recursive functions can be implemented using a helper that delays computation until final result is needed.
claims.evidence: const factorialWithDelayedWork = (n, work) => n === 1 ? work : factorialWithDelayedWork(n - 1, n * work);

claims.claim: Destructuring assignment supports default values for variables.
claims.evidence: const [first, second = "two"] = ["one"];

claims.claim: Destructuring parameters can have default values just like regular parameters.
claims.evidence: const length = ([first, ...rest], numberToBeAdded = 0) => first === undefined ? numberToBeAdded : length(rest, 1 + numberToBeAdded)

claims.claim: Functional programming techniques can be applied to data structures by mirroring their self-similarity in recursive algorithms.
claims.evidence: it is an extremely simple illustration of how recursion works when you have a self-similar means of constructing a data structure.

claims.claim: Recursive algorithms that mirror data structure composition can be inefficient when using array destructuring due to repeated copying.
claims.evidence: Our [first, ...rest] approach to recursion is slow because that it creates a lot of temporary arrays, and it spends an enormous amount of time copying elements into arrays that end up being discarded.

claims.claim: A function can be defined to delay work by using a delayed parameter to accumulate results in reverse order.
claims.evidence: const copy2 = (node, delayed = EMPTY) => node === EMPTY ? delayed : copy2(node.rest, { first: node.first, rest: delayed });

claims.claim: A reverse function can be implemented using tail recursion with an accumulator to build the reversed list.
claims.evidence: const reverse = (node, delayed = EMPTY) => node === EMPTY ? delayed : reverse(node.rest, { first: node.first, rest: delayed });

claims.claim: A reverseMapWith function applies a transformation to each element and builds the result in reverse order.
claims.evidence: const reverseMapWith = (fn, node, delayed = EMPTY) => node === EMPTY ? delayed : reverseMapWith(fn, node.rest, { first: fn(node.first), rest: delayed });

claims.claim: A mapWith function can be implemented by applying a transformation and then reversing the result to maintain original order.
claims.evidence: const mapWith = (fn, node, delayed = EMPTY) => node === EMPTY ? reverse(delayed) : mapWith(fn, node.rest, { first: fn(node.first), rest: delayed });

claims.claim: JavaScript arrays and objects can be mutated, changing their structure while maintaining the same identity.
claims.evidence: In JavaScript, almost every type of value can _mutate_. Their identities stay the same, but not their structure. Specifically, arrays and objects can mutate.

claims.claim: Multiple variables can reference the same array or object, allowing mutations to affect all references.
claims.evidence: We have established that JavaScript's semantics allow for two different bindings to refer to the same value.

claims.claim: Mutating a shared array or object through one reference affects all references to that value.
claims.evidence: halloween[0] = 2013; })(allHallowsEve); allHallowsEve _//=> [2013, 10, 31]_

claims.claim: Declaring a variable with const prevents rebinding but not mutation of the value.
claims.evidence: Note well: Declaring a variable const does not prevent us from mutating its value, only from rebinding its name.

claims.claim: Linked lists can share structure between different list instances, leading to unintended side effects when mutating.
claims.evidence: Changes made to ThreeToFive affect OneToFive, because they share the same structure.

claims.claim: Structure sharing in linked lists allows efficient operations like taking the rest of a list without copying.
claims.evidence: Structure sharing like this is what makes linked lists so fast for taking everything but the first item of a list: We aren't making a new list, we're using some of the old list.

claims.claim: Destructuring arrays creates copies of elements, unlike linked list references which share nodes.
claims.evidence: When you take the rest of an array with destructuring ([first, ...rest]), you are given a _copy_ of the elements of the array.

claims.claim: Modifications to a parent array do not affect its destructured child array.
claims.evidence: parentArray[2] = "three"; childArray[0] = "two"; parentArray _//=> [1,2,"three"]_ childArray _//=> ["two",3]

claims.claim: Modifications to a parent linked list affect its child list due to shared node references.
claims.evidence: parentList.rest.rest.first = "three"; childList.first = "two";

claims.claim: A copy function can be implemented using mutation to efficiently build a new list without double iteration.
claims.evidence: const copy = (node, head = null , tail = null ) => { if (node === EMPTY) { return head; } else if (tail === null ) { const { first, rest } = node; const newNode = { first, rest }; return copy(rest, newNode, newNode); } else { const { first, rest } = node; const newNode = { first, rest }; tail.rest = newNode; return copy(node.rest, head, newNode); } }

claims.claim: A mapWith function can be implemented using mutation to efficiently transform elements while building the result.
claims.evidence: const mapWith = (fn, node, head = null , tail = null ) => { if (node === EMPTY) { return head; } else if (tail === null ) { const { first, rest } = node; const newNode = { first: fn(first), rest }; return mapWith(fn, rest, newNode, newNode); } else { const { first, rest } = node; const newNode = { first: fn(first), rest }; tail.rest = newNode; return mapWith(fn, node.rest, head, newNode); } }

claims.claim: Variables declared with let can be reassigned to different values within the same scope.
Variables declared with const cannot be reassigned, but can be shadowed by a new declaration in a nested scope.
claims.evidence: evenStevens = (n) => { if (n === 0) { return true; } else if (n == 1) { return false; } else { return evenStevens(n - 2); } } _//=> ERROR, evenStevens is read-only_

claims.claim: Shadowing a let variable with const allows reassignment in the original scope.
claims.evidence: (() => { let age = 49; if ( true ) { const age = 50; } age = 51; return age; })() _//=> 51_

claims.claim: Shadowing a const variable with let does not allow reassignment in the original scope.
claims.evidence: (() => { const age = 49; if ( true ) { let age = 50; } age = 52; return age; })() _//=> ERROR: age is read-only_

claims.claim: Variables declared with var are function-scoped, not block-scoped, and are hoisted to the top of the function.
claims.evidence: var age = 49; if ( true ) { var age = 50; } return age; })() _//=> 50_

claims.claim: Using let in loops avoids issues with closures capturing the loop variable by creating a new binding for each iteration.
claims.evidence: introductions[1]('Raganwald') _//=> 'Hello, Raganwald, my name is Friedrich'_

claims.claim: A utility function can be defined to retrieve an element at a specific index from a list.
claims.evidence: const at = (index, list) => index === 0 ? first(list) : at(index - 1, rest(list));

claims.claim: A utility function can be defined to set a value at a specific index in a list.
claims.evidence: const set = (index, value, list, originalList = list) => index === 0 ? (list.first = value, originalList) : set(index - 1, value, rest(list), originalList)

claims.claim: When taking the rest of an array with destructuring, you receive a copy of the elements rather than the original elements.
claims.evidence: When you take the rest of an array with destructuring ([first, ...rest]), you are given a _copy_ of the elements of the array.

claims.claim: When taking the rest of a linked list with its reference, you receive the exact same nodes rather than copies.
claims.evidence: When you take the rest of a linked list with its reference, you are given the exact same nodes of the elements of the original list.

claims.claim: Modifications to a parent array do not affect a child array created via destructuring because the child is a copy.
claims.evidence: And therefore, modifications to the parent do not affect the child, and modifications to the child do not affect the parent.

claims.claim: Modifications to a parent linked list affect a child list because they share nodes.
claims.evidence: And therefore, modifications to the parent also modify the child, and modifications to the child also modify the parent.

claims.claim: The rest function can be implemented to make a copy of the rest of a list to avoid structure sharing issues.
claims.evidence: const rest = ({first, rest}) => copy(rest);

claims.claim: The set function can be implemented to create a new list structure instead of modifying existing nodes.
claims.evidence: const set = (index, value, list) => index === 0 ? { first: value, rest: list.rest } : { first: list.first, rest: set(index - 1, value, list.rest) };

claims.claim: Copy-on-write is a strategy where copies are made only when modifications are about to occur.
claims.evidence: This strategy of waiting to copy until you are writing is called copy-on-write, or "COW:"

claims.claim: Copy-on-write is more efficient than copy-on-read when making infrequent small changes.
claims.evidence: Like all strategies, it makes a tradeoff: It's much cheaper than pessimistically copying structures when you make an infrequent number of small changes, but if you tend to make a lot of changes to some that you aren't sharing, it's more expensive.

claims.claim: Copy-on-write avoids unnecessary copying during traversal operations like mapWith.
claims.evidence: And now functions like mapWith that make copies without modifying anything, work at full speed.

claims.claim: An iterator is a function that returns objects with done and value properties.
claims.evidence: A function that takes an iterator and returns another iterator. We can start with take, an easy function that returns an iterator that only returns a fixed number of elements:

claims.claim: Mapping an iterator applies a function to each value as it is retrieved.
claims.evidence: const mapIteratorWith = (fn, iterator) => () => { const {done, value} = iterator(); return ({done, value: done ? undefined : fn(value)}); }

claims.claim: Filtering an iterator applies a predicate to each value and only returns values that satisfy the predicate.
claims.evidence: const filterIteratorWith = (fn, iterator) => () => { do { const {done, value} = iterator(); } while (!done && !fn(value)); return {done, value}; }

claims.claim: The K combinator creates a constant function that always returns the same value regardless of input.
claims.evidence: const K = (x) => (y) => x;

claims.claim: The I combinator returns its argument unchanged.
claims.evidence: const I = (x) => (x);

claims.claim: The V combinator is a function that takes three arguments and applies the third to the results of applying the first and second to the third.
claims.evidence: const V = (x) => (y) => (z) => z(x)(y);

claims.claim: mapWith is a curried function that takes a function first and a list second, reversing the argument order of the standard map function.
claims.evidence: const mapWith = (fn) => (list) => list.map(fn);

claims.claim: mapWith allows creating specialized functions by partially applying the mapping function, such as creating a squaresOf function.
claims.evidence: const squaresOf = mapWith(n => n * n);

claims.claim: flipAndCurry is a higher-order function that takes a function and returns a new function with flipped and curried arguments.
claims.evidence: const flipAndCurry = (fn) => (first) => (second) => fn(second, first);

claims.claim: flip is a higher-order function that takes a function and returns a new function with reversed argument order.
claims.evidence: const flip = (fn) => (first, second) => fn(second, first);

claims.claim: The flip function can be modified to support both curried and non-curried calling patterns based on the number of arguments provided.
claims.evidence: const flip = (fn) => function (first, second) { if (arguments.length === 2) { return fn(second, first); } else { return function (second) { return fn(second, first); }; }; };

claims.claim: flipAndCurry can be used to create mapWith by passing the built-in map function to it.
claims.evidence: var mapWith = flipAndCurry(map);

claims.claim: Object.assign can be used to copy properties from one object to another, enabling object extension and cloning.
claims.evidence: Object.assign(inventory, shipment)

claims.claim: Object.assign can be used to initialize object properties by extending an empty object with source properties.
claims.evidence: Object.assign({}, { apples: 12, oranges: 12 })

claims.claim: An iterator object has a next() method that returns an object with done and value properties.
claims.evidence: return { next () { if (iterationIndex > this.index) { iterationIndex = this.index; } if (iterationIndex < 0) { return {done: true }; } else { return {done: false , value: this.array[iterationIndex--]} } } }

claims.claim: The Symbol.iterator property is a special symbol used to define the method that returns an iterator object for an iterable.
claims.evidence: Symbol.iterator evaluates to a special symbol representing the name of the method that objects should use if they return an iterator object.

claims.claim: The for...of loop works directly with iterable objects that have a Symbol.iterator method returning an iterator object.
claims.evidence: The for...of loop works directly with any object that is _iterable_, meaning it works with any object that has a Symbol.iterator method that returns an object iterator.

claims.claim: Functional programming involves composing functions out of common pieces rather than building them entirely from scratch.
claims.evidence: Both patterns take us to the same destination: Composing functions out of common pieces, rather than building them entirely from scratch.

claims.claim: Quasi-literal strings (template strings) allow embedding expressions using the ${} syntax which are evaluated and converted to strings.
claims.evidence: A quasi-literal can contain an expression to be evaluated. Old-school lispers call this "unquoting," the more contemporary term is "interpolation." An unquoted expression is inserted in a quasi-literal with ${expression}.

claims.claim: Quasi-literal strings are computationally equivalent to string concatenation expressions but are more readable and less error-prone.
claims.evidence: A quasi-literal is computationally equivalent to an expression using +. So the above expression could also be written: 'A popular number for nerds is ' + (40 + 2)

claims.claim: JavaScript iterators are objects with a next() method that returns an object with done and value properties.
claims.evidence: return { next () { if (iterationIndex > this.index) { iterationIndex = this.index; } if (iterationIndex < 0) { return {done: true }; } else { return {done: false , value: this.array[iterationIndex--]} } } } });

claims.claim: An iterator's next() method returns an object indicating whether iteration is complete and the current value.
claims.evidence: return {done: false , value: this.array[iterationIndex--]}

claims.claim: The Symbol.iterator symbol is used to define a method that returns an iterator object for an iterable.
claims.evidence: Symbol.iterator evaluates to a special symbol representing the name of the method that objects should use if they return an iterator object.

claims.claim: Iterables are objects that implement the Symbol.iterator method which returns an iterator.
claims.evidence: any object that has a Symbol.iterator method that returns an object iterator

claims.claim: The for...of loop works directly with any object that implements the Symbol.iterator method.
claims.evidence: The for...of loop works directly with any object that is _iterable_ , meaning it works with any object that has a Symbol.iterator method that returns an object iterator.

claims.claim: The spread operator can be used to spread elements from any iterable into an array literal.
claims.evidence: Now is the time to note that we can spread any iterable. So we can spread the elements of an iterable into an array literal:

claims.claim: The spread operator can also be used to spread elements of an iterable into function parameters.
claims.evidence: And we can also spread the elements of an array literal into parameters:

claims.claim: Spreading an infinite iterable will cause an infinite loop because JavaScript tries to create an array of all elements.
claims.evidence: Attempting to spread an infinite iterable into an array is always going to fail.

claims.claim: Ordered collections are iterables where every iteration produces elements in the same order.
claims.evidence: The iterables we're discussing represent _ordered collections_ . One of the semantic properties of an ordered collection is that every time you iterate over it, you get its elements in order, from the beginning.

claims.claim: Operations on ordered collections return another ordered collection that preserves the iteration semantics.
claims.evidence: Many operations on ordered collections return another ordered collection. They do so by taking care to iterate over a result freshly every time we get an iterator for them.

claims.claim: A collection operation preserves the semantics of the original iterable by creating a new iterator each time.
claims.evidence: Every time we write for (const i of Evens), JavaScript calls Evens[Symbol.iterator](). That in turns means it executes const iterator = Numbers[Symbol.iterator](); every time we write for (const i of Evens), and that means that iterator starts at the beginning of Numbers.

claims.claim: The mapWith function creates a new iterable that applies a transformation to each element of an input iterable.
claims.evidence: const mapWith = (fn, collection) => ({ [Symbol.iterator] () { const iterator = collection[Symbol.iterator](); return { next () { const {done, value} = iterator.next(); return ({done, value: done ? undefined : fn(value)}); } } } });

claims.claim: The filterWith function creates a new iterable that includes only elements matching a predicate from an input iterable.
claims.evidence: const filterWith = (fn, iterable) => ({ [Symbol.iterator] () { const iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); return { next () { do { const {done, value} = iterator.next(); } while (!done && !fn(value)); return {done, value}; } } } });

claims.claim: The untilWith function creates a new iterable that stops iteration when a condition is met.
claims.evidence: const untilWith = (fn, iterable) => ({ [Symbol.iterator] () { const iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); return { next () { let {done, value} = iterator.next(); done = done || fn(value); return ({done, value: done ? undefined : value}); } } } });

claims.claim: The first function retrieves the first element of an iterable by calling next() on its iterator.
claims.evidence: const first = (iterable) => iterable[Symbol.iterator]().next().value;

claims.claim: The rest function returns an iterable that excludes the first element of the input iterable.
claims.evidence: const rest = (iterable) => ({ [Symbol.iterator] () { const iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); iterator.next(); return iterator; } });

claims.claim: The Array.from method can convert any iterable into an array.
Custom collections can provide a .from method to create instances from an iterable.
claims.evidence: Stack3.from = function (iterable) { const stack = this(); for (let element of iterable) { stack.push(element); } return stack; }

claims.claim: Generators are functions that can be paused and resumed, yielding values one at a time.
claims.evidence: When we invoke the function, we get an iterator object back. Let's start with the degenerate example, the empty iterator:

claims.claim: Generator functions are declared with the function* syntax and use the yield keyword to produce values.
claims.evidence: We declare the function using the function * syntax. Not a fat arrow. Not a plain function.

claims.claim: Yield statements in generators pause execution and return a value to the caller.
claims.evidence: We don't return values or output them to console.log. We "yield" values using the yield keyword.

claims.claim: Generators can be used to create iterators that are easier to write than traditional iterative approaches.
claims.evidence: In a generator, we write "do this, then this, then this." In an iterator, we have to wrap that up and explicitly keep track of what step we're on.

claims.claim: Generator functions are declared using the function * syntax and use yield keywords to produce values instead of return statements.
claims.evidence: function * empty () {};

claims.claim: Generator functions can take arguments and yield values which are returned as objects with done and value properties when .next() is called.
claims.evidence: function * only (something) { yield something; }; only("you").next()

claims.claim: Calling a generator function returns an iterator object that can be invoked with .next() to yield values one at a time.
claims.evidence: only("you").next() //=> {"done": false , value: "you"}

claims.claim: Generators behave as coroutines, allowing suspension and resumption of execution at yield points.
claims.evidence: When we call oneTwoThree() and get an iterator.

claims.claim: When a generator yields a value, it suspends execution and returns an object with done and value properties.
claims.evidence: The iterator wraps 1 in {done: false, value: 1} and returns that from the call to .next().

claims.claim: Generator functions can be used to create infinite sequences by using loops and yield statements without explicit termination conditions.
claims.evidence: const Numbers = { [Symbol.iterator] () { let i = 0; while ( true ) { yield i++; } } };

claims.claim: Generator functions can be used recursively to traverse nested structures like trees by yielding elements from sub-iterables.
claims.evidence: for (const ee of tree(e)) { yield ee; }

claims.claim: The yield* syntax allows a generator to delegate yielding to another iterable, effectively flattening nested iterables.
claims.evidence: yield * iterable;

claims.claim: Generator functions simplify implementation of iterable operations compared to manually creating iterator objects.
claims.evidence: function * mapWith (fn, iterable) { for (const element of iterable) { yield fn(element); } }

claims.claim: An iterator is an object with a next() method that returns values in sequence until it is done.
claims.evidence: empty().next() //=> {"done": true }

claims.claim: Iterators produced by generator functions can be consumed using a for...of loop or by repeatedly calling .next().
claims.evidence: for (const i of ThreeNumbers) { console.log(i); }

claims.claim: An iterable object has a Symbol.iterator method that returns an iterator when invoked.
claims.evidence: const ThreeNumbers = { [Symbol.iterator]: function * () { yield 1; yield 2; yield 3 } }

claims.claim: Generators provide a concise way to define iterable objects by implementing the [Symbol.iterator] method with generator syntax.
claims.evidence: const ThreeNumbers = { *[Symbol.iterator] () { yield 1; yield 2; yield 3 } }

claims.claim: Iterables created with generators can be used in for...of loops, spread into arrays, or passed to functions.
claims.evidence: for (const i of ThreeNumbers) { console.log(i); } //=> 1 2 3 [...ThreeNumbers] //=> [1,2,3]

claims.claim: The Symbol.iterator property defines how an object should be iterated over, returning an iterator.
claims.evidence: const ThreeNumbers = { [Symbol.iterator]: function * () { yield 1; yield 2; yield 3 } }

claims.claim: Iterators maintain internal state during iteration and resume execution from where they left off upon subsequent calls to .next().
claims.evidence: The iterator _resumes execution_ from the point where it yielded the last value.

claims.claim: Generator functions use the function * syntax and support yield expressions to produce sequences of values.
claims.evidence: function * empty () {};

claims.claim: Generator methods in objects can be declared using the * syntax for [Symbol.iterator] to make objects iterable.
claims.evidence: const ThreeNumbers = { *[Symbol.iterator] () { yield 1; yield 2; yield 3 } }

claims.claim: The yield* syntax enables delegation of yielding to another iterable, simplifying nested iteration logic.
claims.evidence: yield* tree(e);

claims.claim: Lazy evaluation means computations are deferred until their results are actually needed.
claims.evidence: "Laziness" is the characteristic of not doing any work until you know you need the result of the work.

claims.claim: Lazy collections avoid creating intermediate data structures, reducing memory usage during transformations.
claims.evidence: This reduces the memory footprint. When working with very large collections and many operations, this can be important.

claims.claim: An array can be used to represent the state of a tic-tac-toe game board where each position is stored as an element in the array.
A tic-tac-toe board can be represented as an array of nine elements corresponding to positions on the board.
A plain old JavaScript object (POJO) can be used as a lookup table to map board states to moves.
A stateless function can be used to determine the next move in a tic-tac-toe game based on the current board state.
claims.evidence: statelessNaughtsAndCrosses([ 'o', 'x', ' ', ' ' ' ' ' ' , , , 'o', 'x', ' ' ]) //=> 3

claims.claim: A stateful function can be created by wrapping a stateless function to manage game state internally.
claims.evidence: const statefulNaughtsAndCrosses = () => { const state = [ ' ' ' ' ' ' , , , ' ' ' ' ' ' , , , ' ' ' ' ' ' , , ]; return (x = false ) => { if (x) { if (state[x] === ' ') { state[x] = 'x'; } else throw "occupied!" } let o = moveLookupTable[state]; state[o] = 'o'; return o; } };

claims.claim: Generator functions can be used to create interactive games where moves are passed in and responses are yielded.
claims.evidence: function* generatorNaughtsAndCrosses () { const x1 = yield 0; switch (x1) { case 1: const x2 = yield 6; switch (x2) { case 2: case 4: case 5: case 7: case 8: yield 3; break; case 3: const x3 = yield 8; switch (x3) { case 2: case 5: case 7: yield 4; break; case 4: yield 7; break; } } break; } }

claims.claim: Generator functions can be used to implement state machines where state is maintained implicitly in control flow.
claims.evidence: Our generator function maintains state implicitly in its control flow, but returns an iterator that we call, it doesn't call us.

claims.claim: A generator function can be used to implement an interactive game where moves are passed to .next() and responses are returned.
claims.evidence: aNaughtsAndCrossesGame.next(1).value //=> 6

claims.claim: Operations on iterables include transformations such as mapping, filtering, and taking specific elements.
claims.evidence: function* mapWith(fn, iterable) { for ( const element of iterable) { yield fn(element); } } function* mapAllWith (fn, iterable) { for ( const element of iterable) { yield* fn(element); } } function* filterWith (fn, iterable) { for ( const element of iterable) { if (!!fn(element)) yield element; } } function* compact (iterable) { for ( const element of iterable) { if (element != null ) yield element; } } function* untilWith (fn, iterable) { for ( const element of iterable) { if (fn(element)) break; yield fn(element); } } function* rest (iterable) { const iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); iterator.next(); yield* iterator; } function* take (numberToTake, iterable) { const iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); for ( let i = 0; i < numberToTake; ++i) { const { done, value } = iterator.next(); if (!done) yield value; } }

claims.claim: Zip operations combine multiple iterables into a single iterable by pairing corresponding elements.
claims.evidence: function* zip (...iterables) { const iterators = iterables.map(i => i[Symbol.iterator]()); while ( true ) { const pairs = iterators.map(j => j.next()), dones = pairs.map(p => p.done), values = pairs.map(p => p.value); if (dones.indexOf( true ) >= 0) break; yield values; } }; function* zipWith (zipper, ...iterables) { const iterators = iterables.map(i => i[Symbol.iterator]()); while ( true ) { const pairs = iterators.map(j => j.next()), dones = pairs.map(p => p.done), values = pairs.map(p => p.value); if (dones.indexOf( true ) >= 0) break; yield zipper(...values); } };

claims.claim: Reduce operations transform an iterable into a single value by applying a function cumulatively.
claims.evidence: const reduceWith = (fn, seed, iterable) => { let accumulator = seed; for ( const element of iterable) { accumulator = fn(accumulator, element); } return accumulator; };

claims.claim: Memoization can be applied to generator functions to cache results and avoid recomputation.
claims.evidence: function memoize (generator) { const memos = {}, iterators = {}; return function* (...args) { const key = JSON.stringify(args); let i = 0; if (memos[key] == null ) { memos[key] = []; iterators[key] = generator(...args); } while ( true ) { if (i < memos[key].length) { yield memos[key][i++]; } else { const { done, value } = iterators[key].next(); if (done) { return ; } else { yield memos[key][i++] = value; } } } } }

claims.claim: A function can be defined using the 'function' keyword followed by a name, parameters, and a body.
claims.evidence: for (const ee of tree(e)) { yield ee; }

claims.claim: An object can be created using literal notation with key-value pairs.
JavaScript supports various data types including strings, numbers, booleans, null, undefined, and objects.
claims.evidence: JavaScript, Ruby, CoffeeScript and Java applications scaling out to millions of users

claims.claim: The 'yield' keyword is used in generator functions to produce a sequence of values.
claims.evidence: for (const ee of tree(e)) { yield ee; }

claims.claim: Generator functions use the 'function*' syntax and can contain 'yield' expressions.
claims.evidence: for (const ee of tree(e)) { yield ee; }
